quiz_questions.question_text: What type of bond forms between a metal and a nonmetal?

options.text: Covalent bond
Metallic bond
Ionic bond
Hydrogen bond

quiz_questions.explanation: Ionic bonds form between metals and nonmetals. The metal transfers electrons to the nonmetal, creating oppositely charged ions that attract each other.

quiz_questions.question_text: What type of bond forms between two nonmetals?

options.text: Ionic bond
Metallic bond
Covalent bond
Nuclear bond

quiz_questions.explanation: Covalent bonds form between two nonmetals. Instead of transferring electrons, nonmetals share electrons to achieve a stable electron configuration.

quiz_questions.question_text: In an ionic bond, what happens to the electrons?

options.text: Electrons are shared equally between the two atoms
Electrons are transferred from one atom to another
Electrons are destroyed during the bonding process
Electrons move into the nucleus of both atoms here

quiz_questions.explanation: In ionic bonding, one atom (usually a metal) transfers one or more electrons to another atom (usually a nonmetal), creating positively and negatively charged ions.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a covalent bond, what happens to the electrons?

options.text: Electrons are transferred from metal to nonmetal
Electrons are shared between the two bonded atoms
Electrons are removed from both atoms completely
Electrons are absorbed into the nucleus of one atom

quiz_questions.explanation: In covalent bonding, atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Both atoms contribute electrons to the shared pair, and both atoms count the shared electrons as part of their outer shell.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is an example of an ionic compound?

options.text: Water (H2O)
Sodium chloride (NaCl)
Carbon dioxide (CO2)
Methane (CH4)

quiz_questions.explanation: Sodium chloride (NaCl) is ionic because sodium is a metal that transfers its electron to chlorine, a nonmetal. The other compounds listed are covalent because they involve only nonmetals.

quiz_questions.question_text: Do ionic compounds generally have high or low melting points?

options.text: Low melting points compared to most substances
High melting points compared to most substances
They have no definite melting point at all
The same melting points as covalent compounds

quiz_questions.explanation: Ionic compounds generally have high melting points because the strong electrostatic attraction between positive and negative ions requires a lot of energy to overcome.

quiz_questions.question_text: Can ionic compounds conduct electricity when dissolved in water?

options.text: No, they cannot conduct electricity under any conditions
Yes, because the ions are free to move and carry charge
Only if they are heated above their boiling point first
Only if they are mixed with covalent compounds in water

quiz_questions.explanation: When dissolved in water, ionic compounds dissociate into free-moving ions that can carry electrical current. In solid form, the ions are locked in place and cannot conduct.

quiz_questions.question_text: Do most covalent compounds conduct electricity?

options.text: Yes, covalent compounds are excellent conductors always
No, most covalent compounds do not conduct electricity
Only when they are in solid form will they conduct well
Only covalent gases can conduct electricity when heated

quiz_questions.explanation: Most covalent compounds do not conduct electricity because they consist of neutral molecules without free-moving charged particles. There are no ions to carry electrical current.

quiz_questions.question_text: Ionic compounds form organized structures called what?

options.text: Crystal lattices
Molecular chains
Electron clouds
Covalent networks

quiz_questions.explanation: Ionic compounds form crystal lattices -- organized, repeating three-dimensional arrangements of alternating positive and negative ions. This structure gives ionic compounds their characteristic properties.

quiz_questions.question_text: Are covalent compounds generally solid, liquid, or gas at room temperature?

options.text: Always solid at room temperature
Always liquid at room temperature
They can be solid, liquid, or gas
Always gas at room temperature only

quiz_questions.explanation: Covalent compounds can be found in any state at room temperature. Water is liquid, sugar is solid, and carbon dioxide is a gas. Their lower intermolecular forces allow for this variety.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an ionic bond?

options.text: A bond formed by the transfer of electrons from one atom to another creating ions
A bond formed by the sharing of electrons between two nonmetal atoms in a pair
A bond formed by the attraction between positive metal ions and free electrons
A weak bond that exists between molecules rather than within individual molecules

quiz_questions.explanation: An ionic bond is the electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions formed when one atom transfers electrons to another. Typically, a metal loses electrons and a nonmetal gains them.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a covalent bond?

options.text: A bond formed when two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons between them
A bond formed when one atom transfers all its electrons to another atom nearby
A bond that holds metal atoms together through a sea of shared free electrons
A bond formed by the magnetic attraction between two atoms placed near each other

quiz_questions.explanation: A covalent bond forms when two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Both atoms contribute electrons to the bond, and the shared electrons are attracted to both nuclei.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'electrostatic attraction' mean?

options.text: The attractive force between oppositely charged particles like ions
The repulsive force between particles that have the same charge
The force of gravity that pulls all atoms toward Earth's center
The magnetic force that exists between iron atoms in a magnet

quiz_questions.explanation: Electrostatic attraction is the force that draws oppositely charged particles together. In ionic bonds, positive cations and negative anions are held together by this electrostatic attraction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'crystal lattice' mean?

options.text: A regular repeating 3D arrangement of ions in an ionic compound
A random arrangement of molecules in a covalent compound solid
The pattern of electrons orbiting the nucleus of a single atom
A type of laboratory equipment used to grow ionic compound crystals

quiz_questions.explanation: A crystal lattice is the highly organized, three-dimensional repeating pattern in which ions are arranged in an ionic solid. Each positive ion is surrounded by negative ions and vice versa.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a molecule?

options.text: A group of two or more atoms held together by covalent bonds
A single atom that exists by itself without bonding to others
Any substance that is found in the liquid state at room temp
A pair of ions held together by electrostatic attraction force

quiz_questions.explanation: A molecule is a group of two or more atoms held together by covalent bonds. Molecules are the smallest unit of a covalent compound that retains its chemical properties.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'formula unit' mean for ionic compounds?

options.text: The simplest ratio of ions in an ionic compound like NaCl or MgO
A single molecule of an ionic compound floating freely in solution
The total number of atoms present in one crystal of the compound
The equipment formula used to measure ionic compounds in the lab

quiz_questions.explanation: A formula unit is the simplest ratio of ions in an ionic compound. NaCl has a 1:1 ratio of Na+ to Cl-. Ionic compounds do not form discrete molecules but rather extended crystal lattices.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'electron transfer' mean?

options.text: When one atom gives up electrons to another atom during ionic bonding
When two atoms equally share their electrons in a covalent bond made
When electrons move from one energy level to another inside one atom
When electricity flows through a wire made of a conducting metal material

quiz_questions.explanation: Electron transfer is when one or more electrons move completely from one atom to another during ionic bond formation, creating a cation (positive ion) and an anion (negative ion).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'electron sharing' mean?

options.text: When two atoms both contribute electrons to a bond and share them
When one atom takes all the electrons from the other atom entirely
When electrons are evenly distributed across all atoms in a sample
When electrons leave both atoms and exist freely between molecules

quiz_questions.explanation: Electron sharing occurs in covalent bonds where two atoms both contribute electrons to a shared pair. The shared electrons are attracted to the nuclei of both atoms.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a polyatomic ion?

options.text: A charged group of covalently bonded atoms that acts as a single unit
A single atom that has gained or lost electrons to form a charged ion
A neutral molecule made up of three or more different types of atoms
Two ions that are bonded together forming a simple ionic compound pair

quiz_questions.explanation: A polyatomic ion is a group of atoms that are covalently bonded together but carry an overall charge. Examples include hydroxide (OH-), sulfate (SO4 2-), and ammonium (NH4+).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'dissociation' mean for ionic compounds?

options.text: The separation of an ionic compound into individual ions when dissolved in water
The formation of an ionic compound from individual metal and nonmetal elements
The breaking of covalent bonds within a molecule to form separate free atoms
The evaporation of an ionic compound from solid state directly into gas phase

quiz_questions.explanation: Dissociation is when an ionic compound separates into its individual ions when dissolved in water. For example, NaCl dissociates into Na+ and Cl- ions in solution.

quiz_questions.question_text: Table salt (NaCl) has a melting point of 801 degrees Celsius, while sugar (C12H22O11) melts at 186 degrees Celsius. What explains this large difference?

options.text: Salt is ionic with strong ion attractions while sugar is covalent with weaker forces
Salt has more atoms per molecule than sugar so it takes more energy to melt it
Sugar is a metal compound while salt is a nonmetal compound making them different
Salt is heavier than sugar so heavier substances always have higher melting points

quiz_questions.explanation: NaCl is an ionic compound with strong electrostatic attractions in its crystal lattice, requiring high temperatures to break apart. Sugar is a covalent compound with weaker intermolecular forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student dissolves salt in water and tests it with a light bulb conductivity tester. The light turns on. Then the student dissolves sugar in water and the light does not turn on. Why?

options.text: Salt water conducts because dissolved ions carry charge while sugar has no ions
Sugar water is too thick for electricity to pass through while salt water is thin
The light bulb was broken during the second test with the sugar water solution
Salt produces heat when dissolved which powers the light while sugar does not

quiz_questions.explanation: Salt (NaCl) is ionic and dissociates into Na+ and Cl- ions in water, which carry electrical current. Sugar is covalent and dissolves as neutral molecules, so it cannot conduct electricity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Carbon dioxide (CO2) is a covalent compound that is a gas at room temperature. Calcium oxide (CaO) is an ionic compound that is a solid with a very high melting point. Both contain oxygen. What causes their different properties?

options.text: They contain different types of oxygen atoms that have different properties
CO2 has covalent bonds with weak intermolecular forces while CaO has strong ionic bonds
CO2 is larger than CaO so it cannot hold together as a solid at room temperature
Calcium is heavier than carbon which automatically makes CaO a solid at room temperature

quiz_questions.explanation: CO2 consists of individual molecules held together by weak intermolecular forces (gas at room temperature). CaO has strong ionic bonds in a crystal lattice requiring enormous energy to break.

quiz_questions.question_text: Magnesium and chlorine react to form MgCl2. Magnesium has 2 valence electrons and chlorine has 7. How does the electron transfer work?

options.text: Magnesium transfers 1 electron to one chlorine atom only in the compound
Each chlorine atom transfers 1 electron to magnesium in the reaction here
Magnesium transfers 1 electron to each of 2 chlorine atoms in the compound
Magnesium and chlorine share electrons equally forming a covalent bond pair

quiz_questions.explanation: Magnesium loses its 2 valence electrons, giving 1 to each of 2 chlorine atoms. Magnesium becomes Mg2+ and each chlorine becomes Cl-. This creates the ionic compound MgCl2.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is given an unknown white solid. It dissolves in water and the solution conducts electricity. It also has a high melting point. Is it likely ionic or covalent?

options.text: Covalent, because it dissolves in water and is a white solid substance
Ionic, because it conducts when dissolved and has a high melting point
Neither, because only metals conduct electricity in water solutions here
Both, because all compounds are both ionic and covalent at the same time

quiz_questions.explanation: Conducting electricity when dissolved in water and having a high melting point are both characteristic of ionic compounds. The ions dissociate in water and carry charge.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student asks why water (a covalent compound) is liquid at room temperature while many other covalent compounds are gases. What is the best explanation?

options.text: Water has strong hydrogen bonds between its molecules keeping it liquid
Water is actually an ionic compound which is why it is liquid at room temp
Water molecules are heavier than all other covalent compounds' molecules
Water has metallic bonds that make it a liquid at standard room temperature

quiz_questions.explanation: Water is liquid at room temperature because of strong hydrogen bonds between water molecules. These intermolecular forces are stronger than those in most covalent compounds, keeping water in the liquid state.

quiz_questions.question_text: Sodium has 1 valence electron and oxygen has 6. When they form an ionic compound, what is the correct formula?

options.text: NaO, because one sodium bonds with one oxygen
Na2O, because two sodiums each give one electron to oxygen
NaO2, because sodium needs two oxygens to be stable
Na6O, because oxygen has 6 valence electrons to fill

quiz_questions.explanation: Oxygen needs 2 electrons to complete its octet, but each sodium can only give 1. So 2 sodium atoms each transfer 1 electron to oxygen, making the formula Na2O.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student holds an ice cube (solid water) and a salt crystal. Both are solid, but the student knows they are very different types of compounds. How could the student test which is ionic?

options.text: Dissolve each in water and test if the solution conducts electricity with a circuit
Weigh each sample because ionic compounds are always heavier than covalent ones
Look at the color because ionic compounds are always white and covalent are clear
Smell each sample because ionic compounds have strong odors and covalent do not

quiz_questions.explanation: Dissolving in water and testing conductivity is a reliable way to distinguish ionic from covalent compounds. The salt solution will conduct electricity (ions present) while sugar water will not.

quiz_questions.question_text: Calcium fluoride (CaF2) is used in toothpaste. Calcium is a metal with 2 valence electrons and fluorine is a nonmetal with 7. What type of bonding holds this compound together?

options.text: Covalent bonding because both elements need electrons for their outer shell
Metallic bonding because calcium is a metal in the alkaline earth group
Ionic bonding because a metal transfers electrons to nonmetal atoms here
Hydrogen bonding because fluorine is involved and it bonds with hydrogen

quiz_questions.explanation: CaF2 is held together by ionic bonds. Calcium (metal) transfers its 2 valence electrons, one to each of 2 fluorine atoms (nonmetals), forming Ca2+ and 2 F- ions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Diamond is made entirely of carbon atoms bonded together. Is diamond an ionic or covalent substance, and what property gives evidence for your answer?

options.text: Ionic, because diamond is very hard and only ionic compounds can be hard
Covalent, because carbon atoms share electrons and diamond does not conduct
Ionic, because diamond has a very high melting point like all ionic substances
Neither, because diamond is an element and only compounds can have bonds

quiz_questions.explanation: Diamond is a covalent network solid. Carbon atoms share electrons with each other (covalent bonds). Evidence includes that diamond does not conduct electricity, which is typical of covalent substances.
